hypothesis: Defer every is_reduce_only order by 50 ms via a clock alert so residual oracle drift accrues to the closed position's realized P&L. Opening orders pass through unchanged.
notes: Train P&L delta is -0.77% vs the +5% gate (FAIL, not CLOSE; gap=5.77pp > close_margin_pct=2.0). Slippage uninformative — fill model reports 0.0 for both algos on every date (see research/NOTES.md). Win-rate rose +6-9pp under the defer but mean dollar P&L did not, suggesting the defer redistributes outcomes without enlarging them. Trade count drops ~25% because some deferred closes get superseded by later strategy reversals — the algo isn't a pure scheduling overlay. Test split is only 1 date (cached only — see RESULT WARNING in research/NOTES.md); not a basis for OOS claims either way.

hypothesis: Filter opening orders by top-of-book bid/ask imbalance (threshold=0.45): submit a BUY open only if bid_size/(bid_size+ask_size) >= 0.45, and a SELL open only if ask_size/(bid_size+ask_size) >= 0.45. Reduce-only (closing) orders always pass through immediately. Filtered opens are dropped, allowing the oracle to re-enter on the next signal cycle (~1s) if the forecast persists.
notes: Train P&L delta is -0.368% vs the +5% gate (FAIL; gap=5.368pp > close_margin_pct=2.0). Slippage uninformative — fill model reports 0.0 for both algos (see research/NOTES.md). Win rate improved consistently (+0.9pp, +3.2pp, +6.9pp across train dates) confirming the filter removes some losing trades; but at threshold=0.45 it also removes enough winning trades to produce net P&L loss on 2 of 3 train dates. Only 20260317 saw positive delta (+1.8%). Test date (1 day only — see RESULT WARNING) showed +0.764%. The mechanism shows promise on high-volume days; a stronger threshold (0.55+) or a volatility-conditional threshold may unlock more consistent P&L improvement. Same 4 cached partitions used as prior iteration (within budget).
